items.q: What is the difference between ductility and malleability?
items.correct: Ductility is being drawn into wires; malleability is being hammered into sheets.
items.misconception: They mean exactly the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Ductility is for metals, malleability is for plastics.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms because both involve deformation. Ductility = tensile (stretching into wires); malleability = compressive (rolling/hammering into sheets). Some metals excel at one but not the other.
Students use magnetism as the test, but stainless steel (ferrous) can be non-magnetic. The defining property is iron content - 'ferrous' comes from the Latin for iron.

items.q: If voltage is doubled and resistance is unchanged, what happens to the current?
items.correct: Current doubles (V = IR, so I = V/R).
items.why: Students get the direction of proportionality wrong. Current is PROPORTIONAL to voltage (when R is constant) - more push, more flow. They sometimes confuse this with current being inversely proportional to resistance.

items.q: Whose 1995 About Face popularised design personas?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Alan Cooper. “Don Norman” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which ISO standard governs human-centred design process?
items.why: It's ISO 9241-210. “ISO 9001” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was WCAG 2.1 published?
items.why: The real figure is 2018 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
